mnemonics.title: I ACAS PIMP - Core Security Functions
mnemonics.content: I ACAS PIMP: Identification, Authentication, Access Control, Audit, System Integrity, Protection (Data & Communications), Integrity, Media Protection, Management (Security), Physical Protection. These are the fundamental security functions every system must implement.

mnemonics.title: CIA Triad Reminder
mnemonics.content: CIA: Confidentiality (preventing unauthorized disclosure), Integrity (preventing unauthorized modification), Availability (ensuring timely access). All functional requirements aim to protect one or more of these pillars.

mnemonics.title: AAA Services
mnemonics.content: AAA = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting. Authentication verifies WHO you are, Authorization determines WHAT you can do, Accounting tracks WHAT you did (audit trail).

mnemonics.title: Identity Proofing Phrase
mnemonics.content: To remember the identity verification process: 'I See People Waiting At Window' - Identify, Collect evidence, Verify, Proofing, Await approval, Weigh evidence, X-ray (additional checks).

acronyms.usage: Use when discussing systems that manage digital identities and their access rights. Combines authentication, authorization, and user management.
Use when one set of credentials grants access to multiple applications. Improves user experience while maintaining security through centralized authentication.
Use when access decisions are based on user roles within an organization. Simplifies permission management by grouping privileges.
Use when system enforces access rules regardless of user preference. Common in government/military systems with strict classification levels.
Use when owners control who can access their resources. Typical in personal computers and file systems.
Use when referring to data that can identify an individual. Requires specific protection under privacy regulations.
Use when discussing cryptographic systems using asymmetric key pairs. Provides digital certificates and key management.
Use when describing systems that aggregate and analyze security logs for real-time threat detection.
Use when discussing hardware-based cryptographic modules for secure key storage and platform integrity verification.
Use when describing dynamic passwords valid for single authentication sessions. Enhances security over static passwords.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a three-layered fortress: Layer 1 (Something you KNOW - password/key), Layer 2 (Something you HAVE - token/smartphone), Layer 3 (Something you ARE - fingerprint/face). Strong security requires all three layers.

Think of a movie theater: DAC is like owning a theater and choosing who enters, RBAC is like having ticket types (silver/gold/platinum) with different perks, MAC is like strict security checkpoints with clearance levels.
Imagine CCTV cameras recording every action in a building. Each camera timestamp logs WHO did WHAT, WHEN, and WHERE. Security analysts review this footage to detect suspicious activity.
Visualize an onion with multiple layers. If one layer is breached (attacker gets through perimeter), additional layers (internal firewalls, access controls, encryption) protect the core data.
Think of a hotel key card. A housekeeping card only opens guest rooms and service areas, not the manager's office or server room. Users get only the minimum access needed for their job.
Imagine two keys needed to launch a nuclear missile - neither person alone can do it. Critical actions require multiple authorized individuals to prevent fraud and error.

analogies.analogy: Identification is showing your ID card at airport security (stating who you are). Authentication is the security guard verifying your ID matches your face (proving you are who you claim). Authorization is the boarding pass showing which gate and class you can access (what you're allowed to do).
A firewall is like a security guard at a building entrance checking visitor credentials against an approved list, only allowing those who meet criteria to enter while blocking suspicious individuals.

Encryption is like writing a secret code (like pig Latin) that only someone who knows the rule can understand. Even if someone intercepts the message, it appears as meaningless gibberish without the decryption key.

A security audit is like a health checkup for your system. Just as a doctor checks vital signs, runs tests, and provides a health report, a security audit examines controls, identifies vulnerabilities, and recommends improvements.

Backups are like having a photocopy of important documents stored in a safety deposit box. If the original is lost or damaged (ransomware attack, hardware failure), you can retrieve the copy to restore operations.

Zero Trust is like airport security where every passenger is treated as potentially suspicious regardless of their frequent flyer status. Everyone must verify identity at every checkpoint, not just once at entry.
